full_name: Ministry of Social Development and Poverty Reduction
blurb: Supports people in need — income and disability assistance, employment supports through WorkBC, accessibility, and poverty reduction.

goals.title: GOAL 1
goals.short_name: British Columbians in need have access to income and disability assistance
goals.description: The ministry delivers BC Employment and Assistance — income assistance, disability assistance for people designated as Persons with Disabilities, and related supplements like the Senior's Supplement and BC Bus Pass — through My Self Serve, local offices, and a toll-free line.

main_services.name: Apply for and manage income assistance

supporting.name: Apply for income assistance
supporting.desc: If you're out of work, waiting on other income, or urgently need food, shelter or medical attention, you may be eligible for BC Employment and Assistance (BCEA). Apply through My Self Serve or your local office; most applicants must complete a work search before an eligibility assessment.
supporting.users: People with no other resources to meet their basic needs

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Income assistance eligibility and application

supporting.name: Manage your income or disability assistance case online
supporting.desc: My Self Serve (MySS) gives income and disability assistance clients secure online access to apply, message the ministry, submit monthly reports, check upcoming payment eligibility, sign employment plans, and track the annual earnings exemption.
supporting.users: Income and disability assistance clients

supporting.name: Get emergency hardship assistance
supporting.desc: If you don't meet the regular eligibility rules for income assistance but are still in urgent need, you may be able to receive hardship assistance depending on your circumstances.
supporting.users: People in urgent financial need who don't meet regular eligibility

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Hardship assistance

supporting.name: Ask for reconsideration of an assistance decision
supporting.desc: If you disagree with a decision affecting your income or disability assistance, you can first speak to a worker about your concerns, then request a formal reconsideration and, if needed, appeal to the Employment and Assistance Appeal Tribunal.
supporting.users: Income and disability assistance applicants and recipients

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Reconsideration and appeal rights

main_services.name: Apply for and manage disability assistance

supporting.name: Apply for the Persons with Disabilities designation
supporting.desc: To receive disability assistance you must be designated as a Person with Disabilities (PWD). The application has three parts — a section you complete, a medical report from a doctor or nurse practitioner, and an assessor report from a prescribed professional.
supporting.users: People with a severe, long-term physical or mental impairment

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance for Persons with Disabilities Act  -  Persons with disabilities designation

supporting.name: Apply for disability assistance
supporting.desc: Disability assistance provides monthly financial and health support to people with the PWD designation who meet income and asset eligibility rules — general asset exemption limits are $100,000 for a single person or couple, and $200,000 for a couple where both partners have the designation.
supporting.users: People with the PWD designation

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance for Persons with Disabilities Act  -  Disability assistance and supplements

supporting.name: Use a simplified application if you already receive disability-related benefits
supporting.desc: People already receiving Community Living BC supports, the MCFD At Home Program, BC PharmaCare Plan P – Palliative Care Benefits, or CPP Disability Benefits can complete a shorter PWD application that lets the ministry confirm eligibility from that program, instead of a new medical assessment.
supporting.users: People already receiving certain disability-related benefits

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance for Persons with Disabilities Act  -  Persons with disabilities designation

supporting.name: Track your annual earnings exemption on disability assistance
supporting.desc: People on disability assistance can work and earn money up to an annual limit without it affecting their monthly payment; My Self Serve lets clients track how much of the exemption they've used.
supporting.users: People on disability assistance who are working

main_services.name: Get financial support as a senior or through the bus pass program

supporting.name: Get a monthly top-up to your federal seniors' pension
supporting.desc: The Senior's Supplement is an automatic monthly payment for low-income seniors receiving Old Age Security and the Guaranteed Income Supplement (or the federal spouse's allowance) that tops up their federal income — no application is needed.
supporting.users: Low-income seniors receiving OAS/GIS

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Senior's Supplement

supporting.name: Get a reduced-cost bus pass as a senior or person with a disability
supporting.desc: The BC Bus Pass Program provides a subsidized annual Compass Card pass, usable on BC Transit and TransLink, to low-income seniors and people receiving disability assistance. Apply, renew, or request a replacement online.
supporting.users: Low-income seniors and people on disability assistance

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  BC Bus Pass Program

goals.title: GOAL 2
goals.short_name: People with disabilities get community living supports and a more accessible B.C.
goals.description: Community Living BC funds supports for adults with developmental disabilities, autism or FASD, while the Accessibility Directorate leads implementation of the Accessible B.C. Act across government.

main_services.name: Get community living and disability supports

supporting.name: Get community living supports as an adult with a developmental disability
supporting.desc: Community Living BC (CLBC), a provincial Crown corporation, funds home sharing, supported and independent living, community inclusion, and employment supports for adults 19 and older with a developmental disability, autism spectrum disorder, or fetal alcohol spectrum disorder who need significant help with daily tasks.
supporting.users: Adults with developmental disabilities, autism, or FASD

legislation.full_name: Community Living Authority Act  -  Establishment of Community Living BC

supporting.name: Request a review of a community living funding decision or file a complaint
supporting.desc: People who disagree with a CLBC funding or eligibility decision can request a review through CLBC's complaints process, and can separately report abuse, neglect, or wrongdoing.
supporting.users: CLBC clients and families

channels.label: Request a Review with the Complaints Process

legislation.full_name: Community Living Authority Act  -  Establishment of Community Living BC

supporting.name: Get transition planning support as a youth with a developmental disability
supporting.desc: A Navigator helps young people with a developmental disability, and their families, plan the move from Ministry of Children and Family Development supports into adult supports, including Community Living BC, as they approach age 19.
supporting.users: Youth with developmental disabilities and their families

channels.label: Transition Planning for Youth & Young Adults

relationships.service: Get financial support for a child with a disability or support needs

supporting.name: Find financial supports for people with disabilities
supporting.desc: A directory of financial supports for people with disabilities to help cover costs of daily living and other essential needs, beyond disability assistance itself.
supporting.users: People with disabilities

main_services.name: Advance accessibility and service quality across government

supporting.name: Give feedback on accessibility barriers in government services
supporting.desc: The Accessibility Directorate's online feedback tool lets people report barriers they face accessing B.C. government services, buildings, and information, and routes feedback to the relevant ministry.
supporting.users: People with disabilities, the public

legislation.full_name: Accessible British Columbia Act  -  Accessibility feedback mechanisms

mandate_tags.text: With support from the Parliamentary Secretary for Accessibility, lead government's work on accessibility initiatives.

supporting.name: Track government's progress on accessibility standards and planning
supporting.desc: The Accessibility Directorate coordinates development of government's accessibility standards, regulations, and accessibility plan under the Accessible B.C. Act, aiming to identify, remove, and prevent barriers for people with disabilities.
supporting.users: General public, disability advocates

legislation.full_name: Accessible British Columbia Act  -  Accessibility standards and government accessibility plan

mandate_tags.text: With support from the Parliamentary Secretary for Accessibility, lead government's work on accessibility initiatives.

supporting.name: Get help resolving a service quality concern as a person with a disability
supporting.desc: B.C.'s Advocate for Service Quality reports directly to the Minister of Social Development and Poverty Reduction and helps resolve concerns about the quality of disability and community living services.
supporting.users: People with disabilities and their families

goals.title: GOAL 3
goals.short_name: British Columbians facing employment barriers find and keep good jobs
goals.description: WorkBC Employment Services Centres, delivered for the ministry, help job seekers — including people on income and disability assistance — find work, with extra training supports for single parents and people facing complex barriers.

main_services.name: Get help finding and keeping a job

supporting.name: Get help finding a job at an employment services centre
supporting.desc: WorkBC Employment Services Centres provide free help with job search, resume writing, interview preparation, and skills training to all British Columbians, including people on income or disability assistance.
supporting.users: Job seekers, income and disability assistance clients

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Employment-related programs

mandate_tags.text: Lead work, with support from the Minister of Post-Secondary Education and Future Skills, to deliver skills and training initiatives for people facing multiple complex barriers that will enable them to access opportunities and build a dignified life.

relationships.service: Search for jobs and labour market information through WorkBC

supporting.name: Get training and support as a single parent to secure long-term employment
supporting.desc: The Single Parent Employment Initiative offers single parents on income or disability assistance up to two years of funded training or paid work experience, continued assistance during training, child care and transportation costs covered, and a year of extended health coverage after starting work.
supporting.users: Single parents on income or disability assistance

legislation.full_name: Employment and Assistance Act  -  Employment-related programs

mandate_tags.text: Lead work, with support from the Minister of Post-Secondary Education and Future Skills, to deliver skills and training initiatives for people facing multiple complex barriers that will enable them to access opportunities and build a dignified life.

supporting.name: Get help finding a job as a person with a disability
supporting.desc: WorkBC offers employment services tailored to people with disabilities, alongside the BC Public Service's Work-Able program, a paid internship for recent post-secondary graduates who self-identify as having a disability.
supporting.users: Job seekers with disabilities

channels.label: WorkBC services for people with disabilities

goals.title: GOAL 4
goals.short_name: Poverty is reduced and British Columbians are more socially included
goals.description: British Columbia's 2024 Poverty Reduction Strategy sets legislated targets to cut overall, child, and seniors' poverty by 2034, tracked through annual progress reports and an independent advisory committee.

main_services.name: Track and shape B.C.'s Poverty Reduction Strategy

supporting.name: Track progress on BC's Poverty Reduction Strategy targets
supporting.desc: British Columbia's 2024 Poverty Reduction Strategy sets legislated targets to cut overall poverty by 60%, child poverty by 75%, and — for the first time — seniors' poverty by 50%, by 2034, with annual progress reports published each year.
supporting.users: General public, community organizations

legislation.full_name: Poverty Reduction Strategy Act  -  Targets, timelines and accountability

mandate_tags.text: Continue to develop tools and approaches to prevent poverty and increase income, focusing first on youth growing up in families living in poverty, and work with the Ministers of Health, Finance, Citizens' Services and others to ensure low- and moderate-income British Columbians receive and retain the federal and provincial benefits they are eligible for, beginning with the Canada Disability Benefit.

supporting.name: Take part in an advisory committee on poverty reduction
supporting.desc: An independent Poverty Reduction Advisory Committee — including advocates, experts, Indigenous Peoples, and people with lived experience of poverty — advises the Minister and publishes its views in each annual progress report.
supporting.users: Advocates, experts, people with lived experience

legislation.full_name: Poverty Reduction Strategy Act  -  Targets, timelines and accountability

supporting.name: Share your experience of poverty in B.C.
supporting.desc: Government periodically engages British Columbians — including a distinctions-based process with Indigenous Peoples — to hear about lived experiences of poverty and shape the Poverty Reduction Strategy.
supporting.users: People with lived experience of poverty, general public

foundational_services.name: Ministry program and funding review
foundational_services.desc: Ongoing review of all Ministry of Social Development and Poverty Reduction programs and initiatives with the Minister of Finance, to keep programs relevant and efficient in the context of provincial budget constraints, community safety, and poverty reduction goals.

mandate_tags.text: In order to protect key services that British Columbians rely on, work with the Minister of Finance to review all existing Ministry of Social Development and Poverty Reduction programs and initiatives to ensure our programs remain relevant, are efficient, ensure safe and strong communities, and reduce poverty. This is important in the context of current Provincial budget constraints and the priorities of communities in the province.

foundational_services.name: Poverty prevention and benefit uptake coordination
foundational_services.desc: Cross-government work with the Ministries of Health, Finance, and Citizens' Services to help low- and moderate-income British Columbians receive and retain the federal and provincial benefits they're eligible for, beginning with the Canada Disability Benefit, and to prevent poverty among youth growing up in low-income families.

mandate_tags.text: Continue to develop tools and approaches to prevent poverty and increase income, focusing first on youth growing up in families living in poverty, and work with the Ministers of Health, Finance, Citizens' Services and others to ensure low- and moderate-income British Columbians receive and retain the federal and provincial benefits they are eligible for, beginning with the Canada Disability Benefit.

foundational_services.name: Food security policy leadership
foundational_services.desc: Leads cross-government work on food security for British Columbians struggling with the cost of food and unable to afford the basics, and looks for opportunities to support B.C. farmers and food producers in that work.

mandate_tags.text: Support British Columbians struggling with the cost of food and unable to afford the basics by leading work on food security and find opportunities to support BC farmers and food producers in this work.

foundational_services.name: Community safety initiative coordination
foundational_services.desc: Works with the Cabinet Committee on Community Safety to ensure initiatives it identifies are prioritized and delivered by the ministry as required.

mandate_tags.text: Work with the Cabinet Committee on Community Safety to ensure that initiatives identified by the committee are prioritized and delivered by your ministry as required.

mandate_legend.text: In order to protect key services that British Columbians rely on, work with the Minister of Finance to review all existing Ministry of Social Development and Poverty Reduction programs and initiatives to ensure our programs remain relevant, are efficient, ensure safe and strong communities, and reduce poverty. This is important in the context of current Provincial budget constraints and the priorities of communities in the province.

Continue to develop tools and approaches to prevent poverty and increase income, focusing first on youth growing up in families living in poverty, and work with the Ministers of Health, Finance, Citizens' Services and others to ensure low- and moderate-income British Columbians receive and retain the federal and provincial benefits they are eligible for, beginning with the Canada Disability Benefit.
